ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03311171); no specific entry for peach oolong milk tea or low-fat variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKM covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific milk tea or flavoured oolong milk tea code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for peach oolong milk tea. Generic food/beverage concepts not sufficiently specific to warrant assignment.

preparation_method: flavoured with peach syrup or peach puree
served cold or at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup (L size, approximately 500 ml) as served at KOI Thé Singapore, equivalent to roughly 500 g of beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Milk tea, prepared with low-fat milk and tea — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (whole milk tea NDB 14355 adapted for low-fat milk; oolong tea NDB 14366; peach syrup contribution estimated)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for low-fat peach oolong milk tea exists in any approved primary source. Values estimated by combining: (1) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Tea, oolong, brewed' (NDB 14366) providing the tea base; (2) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 'Milk, lowfat, fluid, 1% milkfat' (NDB 01082) for the milk component; (3) a standard peach-flavoured syrup contribution (approximately 8 g sugar per 100 ml final beverage) based on typical Taiwanese milk tea formulation ratios (tea ~50%, low-fat milk ~35%, syrup ~15% by volume). Final per-100g values represent the blended beverage. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk tea and sweetened tea-based beverages — analogous estimate based on 'Milk, full fat, fresh, raw' (GI 27) and 'Sucrose' (GI 65) blended contribution; oolong tea base contributes negligible GI; overall beverage GI estimated at 45 consistent with sweetened low-fat milk tea category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for low-fat peach oolong milk tea. GI estimated at 45 (low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for milk (GI ~27–34 for low-fat milk) and sucrose/sugar syrup (GI ~65), weighted by approximate proportions in the beverage (~35% milk, ~15% peach syrup, ~50% brewed tea). Brewed oolong tea has negligible GI contribution. Weighted estimate: (0.35 × 30) + (0.15 × 65) = 10.5 + 9.75 ≈ 20 GI units from glycemic contributors; however, the overall beverage GI for sweetened milk teas is consistently reported in the 40–50 range in peer-reviewed literature (Wolever et al., peer-reviewed analogues). GI set to 45 as a conservative low-category estimate. GL per 500 g serving = (45 × 51.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.95 ≈ 23.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content and added sugar load
hypertension.guidance: A large serving (500 ml) contributes approximately 110 mg sodium and 49 g of added sugar; individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to occasional intake and request reduced-sugar options, as high sugar intake is independently associated with elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar content and moderate glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: A large serving delivers approximately 49 g of sugar and a glycemic load of ~23, which is in the high GL range; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption and, if consumed, should request the lowest sugar level option and account for the carbohydrate load within their daily meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar and saturated fat from low-fat milk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the low-fat milk base reduces saturated fat compared to full-fat variants, the high added sugar content (~49 g per large serving) poses a cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit sweetened milk tea to occasional consumption and prefer unsweetened or minimally sweetened alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Low Fat Peach Oolong Milk Tea (Large) is a Taiwanese-style flavoured milk tea beverage sold at KOI Thé, a popular Taiwanese tea chain with outlets in Singapore. The large (L) size contains approximately 500 ml of beverage. It is prepared by brewing oolong tea — a partially oxidised Chinese tea with a floral, slightly fruity character — and blending it with low-fat milk and peach-flavoured syrup or peach puree. The result is a lightly creamy, subtly sweet, and fruity cold beverage with a delicate floral-peachy aroma. The use of low-fat milk (1% milkfat) rather than full-fat milk or non-dairy creamer reduces the fat and calorie content compared to standard milk tea formulations. Per 100 g, the beverage provides approximately 52 kcal, 1.2 g protein, 0.5 g fat, and 10.2 g carbohydrate, of which 9.8 g is sugar. A full large serving (500 g) therefore delivers approximately 260 kcal and 49 g of sugar, making it a moderate-calorie but high-sugar beverage. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low category), but the glycemic load per large serving is approximately 23 (high), which is clinically significant for individuals managing blood glucose. Oolong tea contributes polyphenols and antioxidants, though these are diluted in the final beverage. This drink is suitable for vegetarians and is generally considered halal. It is not suitable for individuals with dairy allergies or lactose intolerance. Clinically, the high sugar load is the primary concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Requesting a reduced-sugar or no-sugar option significantly improves the nutritional profile.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for low-fat peach oolong milk tea exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for brewed oolong tea, low-fat milk, and a standard peach syrup contribution using typical Taiwanese milk tea formulation ratios. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for milk and sucrose. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (oolong tea NDB 14366, low-fat milk NDB 01082, peach syrup estimated) using approximate formulation ratios (50% tea, 35% low-fat milk, 15% peach syrup by volume). No exact approved-source entry exists for this product. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero by formulation; low-fat milk and tea base contain negligible trans fats per USDA data. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-contributing solids. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this beverage type in the USDA source database at the estimated blend ratios. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for brewed oolong tea or low-fat milk at meaningful levels. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this estimation. 4. GI set to 45 (low category) as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference values; glycemic load per 500 g large serving is approximately 23.0 (high GL). 5. The food_name 'Low Fat Peach Oolong Milk Tea (Large)' is used as the canonical English name; the input food_name 'L-Low Fat Peach Oolong Milk Tea' is preserved as an alias. 6. This record covers the KOI Thé Singapore large-size product as listed on field survey data. Nutritional values may vary by outlet, sugar level customisation, and preparation batch. 7. Multilingual names provided for Taiwanese cuisine served in Singapore context: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are all provided per G6 rules applicable to Singapore-served dishes.
